entry.title: MAD-HTLC
entry.summary:
The security vulnerabilities inherent in using Hash Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs) when one party is a miner are significant and pose real risks. In the scenario described, Bob offers Alice an HTLC, where both parties know different preimages required to unlock the transaction: Alice knows the payment preimage, and Bob knows the bond preimage. After the timelock expires due to Alice not taking any action on the HTLC, Bob broadcasts a refund transaction using his bond preimage. However, if Alice operates as a miner, she could exploit her knowledge of both preimages—her own and the one disclosed by Bob—to attempt to redirect both the payment and bond to herself by mining such a transaction in private.
This attack vector leverages Alice's position as a miner, allowing her to essentially double-spend by not broadcasting her successful theft transaction. The likelihood of Alice successfully executing this theft depends on her control over the network's hashrate; even a minor but significant control can be enough to occasionally succeed in this fraudulent activity. This kind of attack leaves no public evidence if it fails, potentially misleading other network participants about the security and reliability of their transactions. The implications are that while HTLCs offer certain protections and functionalities, they also introduce new risks when interacting with parties who have substantial control over blockchain mining resources.
